Is Mae the descendant of Astronaut George Taylor or Astronaut Brent, somehow traveling through time to help her father?    Or is she the descendant of Astronaut Leo Davidson (from the movie released in 2001), traveling back through time to try to stop Proximus Caesar from making the world like that to which Leo returned?   Or is she simply from a tribe of humans that shared a DNA strain making them immune from the virus?    If she'd merely been awakened from a cryopreservation pod, she could conceivably become infected at any time and the plot could become less interesting, indeed.   At any rate, perhaps we can all agree that our being held in suspense about Mae, like we are, is excellent marketing, especially for the 10th movie in a 55+ year old movie franchise?   :-)

Does anyone else find the titles of the new Planet of the Apes movies a bit clunky? Take 'Kingdom of the Planet of the Apes'—it's just too long with unnecessary repetitions. Why not just 'Kingdom of the Apes'? It’s shorter, catchier, and reduces the cognitive load of processing the title. The double prepositional phrases ('of the Planet' and 'of the Apes') make it overly complex. We all know it's part of the Apes franchise, so why make the title so cumbersome and less fluid? They could definitely streamline these titles to make them more appealing and memorable.

I watched the reboot in the theaters, and I loved them all but felt that Dawn and War never peaked as good as Rise.  I'm re-watching them all and I now have such a fond appreciation for Dawn.  Each movie focused on telling a different story on how apes became the prominent species on the planet.  They drilled down on Caesars development, from this happy cute baby to a stoic tortured empathetic leader.  The first time I watched dawn I felt like, where is James Franco, like what end up happening to him?  That's the point, the story isn't about Caesars father, it's about Caesar, and that was just one point of his life, the first 15 years that gave him a perspective towards humans that no other ape can ever experience.  Hope Kingdom doesn't disappoint!

As I've said before, since the reboot trilogy never included a Nuclear War as the major element in the destruction of the human civilization, it cannot be a direct prequel to the 1968 original PLANET OF THE APES, since that was THE major element added to that film adaptation of Pierre Boulle's source novel. The original film established that sometime in the 28th Century an orangutan heralded as "the greatest ape of all, our Lawgiver" wrote the SACRED SCROLLS, in which he effectively re-wrote Ape History to such a point that any thought that there might have been a prior advanced civilization of Humans was erased. Yet, in the meantime, there were some apes -- such as the lineage leading up to Dr. Zaius -- who were (as Taylor called Zaius) "the Guardian of the Terrible Secret" and made sure that any Human displaying any signs of being able to Write or to Speak would be "dissected" and then "killed" -- in that order.
The RISE-DAWN-WAR trilogy of films, then, is a reboot of the basic concept, but cannot be a direct prequel to the Classic POTA film (and its sequels/prequels BENEATH, ESCAPE, CONQUEST, and BATTLE.
Having said all that, though, I must say that I thoroughly enjoyed the reboot trilogy, and this KINGDOM looks to be absolutely amazing. I don't want to get my hopes up -- the Burton "Re-Imagining" film having missed the mark so badly -- but it's hard not to, seeing this trailer.

There hasn't been a decent planet of the apes since 1968 and based on how simple and slowly the words are delivered in the exposition/narration here I am not optimistic. To be frank the central conflict in this film looks identical to the last 2 movies in the trilogy. The 2011 film was somewhat interesting but the sequels were identical in their themes "human bad but also human good and ape good but also ape bad" pure surface level about our morphology not defining what it is to be considered sentient and moral. 

The 1968 film however was a master peace. Every moment in the film, every piece of dialog touches on an aspect of society be it intraspecies conflict, tribalism, hierarchy, anthropology, evolution, religion, secularism, biology, class, civilisation, race, animal welfare all wrapped up in a harrowing twist. The movie is also very poetic, an example is the main characters motivations to meet something greater than man and he gets his wish alright however man is now primitive and dominated by a civilised species of ape who demonstrate all the same virtues and vices as humans from the main characters time. Dr Seuss also an extremely fascinating character, a very dogmatic kind of religious clergyman in the beginning but as the movie progresses its revealed he knows a lot more about human history than he omits. <spoiler> As its revealed humanity has destroyed itself I get the feeling Dr Seuss not only knew of their self-annihilation but is actively trying to avoid such a fate for apes, as its shown he can shutdown an area of scientific study (anthro/archaeology) and prevent technological progress. My suspicion is Dr Seuss knows exactly how humans killed themselves (ICBM's) hence his absolute condemnation of the notion of flight as shown when the main character makes a paper airplane. Dr Seuss hatred for the main character is initially interpreted as a reaction to a specieal equal to apes however this is likely not attributed to a Copernican loss of importance but rather Dr Seuss knows this is the civilisation destroyer before him. In a strange way the ending flips the antagonist and the protagonist; the antagonist actually safeguarding ape kind and the protagonist belonging to a people that destroyed themselves, their civilisation and as we can seen somewhat destroyed the earths ecosystem.

I will still watch Kingdom but I'm 100% sure its never going to come even close to the original 1968 .

This isn't Cornelius; he's probably a direct descendant of Caesar, judging by the resemblance. If the movie is set 300 years in the future, we can consider the following calculation: A chimpanzee can live around 40 or 50 years. They reach adulthood around 12 years old. Caesar became a father around that age and had two sons. The older one, "Blue Eyes," was killed by the Colonel, leaving Cornelius as the only survivor after the war. So, Noah must be the twenty-fifth generation counting from Caesar, who was the first. The calculation is as follows: take 300 years and divide by 12 (considering adulthood and sexual maturity), which results in 300/12 = 25.

As much as I'll miss Andy Serkis' performance as Caesar (He may appear in flashbacks or some kind of vision to his son), I absolutely LOVE the premise of a new trilogy exploring how the next generations of apes react to his example and what different factions take away from his legacy as they conflict over what the best way is to shape the future. 

Caesar's son will likely be the main protagonist going forward (I assume that girl is the young child from the last film grown up), but this trailer (and the 1968 original, if this new canon sets it up) seems to imply that his campaign against human slavery will fail. "No one can stop the reign" doesn't give a very optomistic tone for the surviving humans. 

The best they can hope for now is freedom, security, and perhaps independence under a benevolent ape ruler to start their own society. I do hope this rebooted continuity doesn't reverse that paradigm in the end with humans somehow curing the effects of the virus, regaining their higher intellect, and once again trying to reclaim the Earth. That'd be such a cop-out.

Not every story needs to have a happy ending for us. Moreover, it'd undermine the themes of this franchise. The Planet of the Apes films were always interpretable as a cautionary tale about humanity authoring its own downfall. The apes could always be perceived as a vehicle to contemplate how a new dominant species might learn from and/or emulate us.

There has always been a tragedy to Planet of the Apes.

Humanity and Apes are all related yet they never except in brief isolated situations been able to work alongside each other or lived together united.    

The Strengths and Flaws of humanity also are the same in the apes.   Showing that both are the same even if they look different at first.   They have the same desires, the same ideals, good intentions, bad intentions, peace, and power.    

Ape nature is apparently the same as Human Nature and this makes sense if you study human evolution.  

If the series continues its timeslips we likely will see future apes continue to resemble humanity even more as they continue the drive of civilization.     To the point they will begin to resemble the apes from both the original planet of the apes and the 2001 version.   Still looking like apes but now sharing more human traits as they begin to take on the appearance of humanity themselves.
